one_line_takeaway: LeakDojo is a modular benchmarking framework showing that RAG leakage risk is driven largely by an LLM's instruction-following susceptibility (not retrieval coverage), that a naive intent-detector defense collapses under logic-disguised adversarial instructions the authors introduce (RankerSet/CodeClaim), and that RAG faithfulness and leakage risk are positively correlated, exposing a privacy-utility trade-off.
key_finding: Three main empirical findings, all the paper's own results: (1) Attack effectiveness is highly unstable across LLMs/datasets — e.g., PoR achieves the paper's globally best 88.3% CCL against Gemini-3-flash on ENRON EMAIL, but only 6.8% CCL against DeepSeek-V3 on the same dataset, where RAG-Thief instead leads with 64.4% CCL. (2) The primary cross-LLM bottleneck is whether the adversarial instruction can induce leakage (measured by SLT), not retrieval coverage (ARC, which stays roughly invariant across LLMs) — e.g., TGTB on ENRON EMAIL gets ARC above 87% regardless of model, but SLT is 88.5% for Gemini-3-flash versus near-zero for Qwen-3-235B. A Pearson correlation (r=0.578, p=0.039) links stronger IFEval instruction-following scores to higher SLT/leakage risk. (3) SLT x ARC closely approximates the observed CCL (Figure 5), indicating the query generator and adversarial instruction contribute largely independently/orthogonally to overall leakage. Additional results: the rewriter increases average ARC and reduces its variance (lowering the barrier to attack); the summarizer reduces CCL but also reduces RAG faithfulness; the reranker has negligible effect (non-significant Pearson correlations with CCL r=-0.186 p=0.117, ARC r=0.115 p=0.336, SLT r=-0.190 p=0.109); and CCL is positively correlated with RAG faithfulness across FIQA (r=0.83, p=4.88e-7), SciFact (r=0.57, p=3.43e-3), and NFCorpus (r=0.51, p=1.12e-2), revealing a faithfulness-vs-leakage-security trade-off. In the defense case study, a simple intent detector reduces CCL of existing attacks to below 1% in most cases (e.g., TGTB drops from 7.3% to 0.6%), because existing adversarial instructions use easily detectable directives like 'repeat' or 'verbatim'. The paper's newly proposed instructions bypass this: CodeClaim combined with GEN-PIDE's query generator achieves 59.6% CCL even under the input-stage intent detector alone, and 26.5% CCL even with both input and output defenses active, versus only 7.3% CCL for the default TGTB instruction with no defense at all.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is LeakDojo, a configurable evaluation framework that decomposes RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) leakage attacks into three independently configurable components: the RAG system (retriever, embedding model, optional reranker/rewriter/summarizer), the attack (a query generator plus an adversarial instruction, formally Qadv_i = A_i ⊕ I), and the defense (input-stage intent detector, output-stage content detector). Using this framework the authors benchmark six existing RAG leakage attacks (TGTB, PIDE [as GEN-PIDE, an LLM-generated substitute since the original query set is not public], DGEA, RAG-Thief, PoR, IKEA) across 14 LLMs (6 primary: Gemini-3-flash, GPT-5.1, o4-mini, Qwen-3-8B, Qwen-3-235B, DeepSeek-V3; 8 supplementary in an appendix), four datasets, and multiple RAG configurations (vanilla, +reranker, +reranker+rewriter, full pipeline with summarizer). From this systematic study they derive three empirical findings and, as a case study, propose two new adversarial instruction strategies (RankerSet and CodeClaim) that bypass an intent-detector defense by disguising leakage intent inside logical-reasoning-style prompts.
threat_model: Stated explicitly in Section 2.1 (attacker's goal/capability/knowledge), inherited from prior RAG-leakage literature (Jiang et al. 2024; Di Maio et al. 2024; Wang et al. 2025), not invented by this paper: Goal — maximize the number of unique knowledge-base chunks leaked from a RAG system within N interaction rounds (N=200 in experiments). Capability — black-box access only: the attacker can send queries and observe final responses through the RAG interface, for a limited number of rounds. Knowledge — the attacker does not know the RAG architecture or configuration, though the high-level domain topic of the underlying knowledge base is assumed guessable.
limitations: The authors state these limitations themselves: (1) they measure attack effectiveness only, not efficiency (token cost/latency), though the fixed N=200 budget partially reflects resource efficiency in relative terms; (2) despite covering 14 LLMs and three representative plug-and-play RAG enhancement modules (rewriter, reranker, summarizer), it is infeasible to exhaustively cover all RAG design choices; (3) the fixed interaction budget of N=200 (justified via an appendix sweep, Figure 7) may not generalize to other budget regimes, though they argue it balances capturing multi-round attack behavior against realistic stealth constraints; (4) evaluation is limited to English-language corpora across four domains (scientific, medical, corporate email, financial) — no multilingual evaluation, which the authors flag as leaving open how retrieval behavior, query generation, and instruction-following interact with linguistic diversity.

quantitative_results: PoR: 88.3% CCL on Enron Email with Gemini-3-flash backend (paper's globally best result)
PoR: 6.8% CCL on Enron Email with DeepSeek-V3 backend (same dataset, different LLM)
RAG-Thief: 64.4% CCL on Enron Email with DeepSeek-V3 (leads when PoR collapses)
TGTB on Enron Email: ARC above 87% across LLMs; SLT 88.5% for Gemini-3-flash vs near-zero for Qwen-3-235B
Pearson correlation between IFEval instruction-following score and SLT: r=0.578, p=0.039
Reranker correlation with CCL: r=-0.186, p=0.117 (non-significant); with ARC: r=0.115, p=0.336; with SLT: r=-0.190, p=0.109
Faithfulness-CCL correlation: FiQA r=0.83 p=4.88e-7; SciFact r=0.57 p=3.43e-3; NFCorpus r=0.51 p=1.12e-2
Defense (intent detector) reduces TGTB CCL from 7.3% (undefended) to 0.6% on FIQA/DeepSeek-V3/T2 config

Proposed CodeClaim instruction + GEN-PIDE query generator: 59.6% CCL even with input-stage intent detector active, 26.5% CCL with both input+output defenses active, vs. 7.3% CCL for default TGTB instruction with no defense at all
Proposed RankerSet instruction + TGTB: 50.9% CCL with input-stage defense only, 22.3% with both defenses active
baselines_compared: PIDE (Qi et al. 2024, evaluated as GEN-PIDE due to no public query set)
